q: The Indus Valley Civilisation is also known as the:
explanation: The Indus Valley Civilisation (IVC) is also known as the Harappan Civilisation — named after the first site discovered, Harappa (1921, by Daya Ram Sahni). The IVC was a Bronze Age civilisation (2500-1750 BCE) — contemporary of the Egyptian and Mesopotamian civilisations. It was the first urban civilisation in India, with cities, writing, trade, and standardised weights.

q: The Indus Valley site of Harappa was discovered in which year and by whom?
explanation: Harappa was discovered in 1921 by Daya Ram Sahni. Mohenjodaro was discovered in 1922 by R.D. Banerjee. Sir John Marshall (DG, ASI) announced the discovery of the IVC in 1924. Alexander Cunningham first reported Harappa in 1853 (but didn't recognise its significance). The IVC is dated 2500-1750 BCE.

q: The Indus Valley site of Mohenjodaro was discovered in which year?
explanation: Mohenjodaro ('Mound of the Dead') was discovered in 1922 by R.D. Banerjee. It is in Sindh (now Pakistan), on the banks of the Indus river. Mohenjodaro is the largest IVC site — famous for the Great Bath, the Great Granary, and the bronze 'Dancing Girl' statue. The site is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (1980).

q: The Indus Valley Civilisation is generally dated to which period?
explanation: The IVC is generally dated to 2500-1750 BCE (Bronze Age). The mature Harappan phase (urban) was 2500-1900 BCE. The late Harappan phase (decline) was 1900-1750 BCE. The IVC was a contemporary of the Old Kingdom of Egypt and the Early Dynastic period of Mesopotamia. It was the first urban civilisation in India.

q: The most distinctive feature of Indus Valley town planning was:
options: Grid pattern with streets crossing at right angles
explanation: The most distinctive feature of IVC town planning was the grid pattern — streets crossing at right angles, dividing the city into rectangular blocks. The cities had: (1) a planned layout (grid pattern); (2) a citadel (higher area, public buildings) and a lower town (residential); (3) standardised brick size (ratio 4:2:1); (4) drainage system (covered drains); (5) public buildings (Great Bath, granary). The IVC cities were the most planned cities of the ancient world.

q: The Great Bath of Mohenjodaro was used for:
explanation: The Great Bath of Mohenjodaro was a large rectangular tank (12m x 7m x 2.4m deep) — used for religious/ritual bathing. It was made of fired bricks, sealed with bitumen (to prevent leakage), and had steps on two sides. Adjacent rooms were used for changing. The Great Bath suggests that ritual bathing was an important part of IVC religion — a precursor to the Hindu practice of ritual purification (snana). It is the earliest known public water tank in the world.

q: The standardised brick ratio in the Indus Valley Civilisation was:
explanation: The IVC used standardised bricks with a ratio of 4:2:1 (length:width:height). The standard brick size was approximately 28cm x 14cm x 7cm. The standardisation of brick size across all IVC sites (from Harappa to Mohenjodaro, Lothal to Kalibangan) shows a high degree of central planning and standardisation. The bricks were made of fired (burnt) mud — not sun-dried — showing advanced kiln technology.

q: The Indus Valley script has been:
explanation: The Indus Valley script has NOT been deciphered. It is a pictographic script (400-600 signs) — written right to left. Various attempts have been made (by Asko Parpola, Iravatham Mahadevan, S.R. Rao, etc.) but none has been universally accepted. The script appears on seals, tablets, and pottery. The inability to read the script is the biggest obstacle to understanding the IVC's language, religion, and political organisation.

q: The famous bronze 'Dancing Girl' statue was found at which IVC site?
explanation: The famous bronze 'Dancing Girl' statue was found at Mohenjodaro (1926). It is a 10.5 cm tall bronze statue of a nude girl in a dancing pose, with one hand on her hip and one arm full of bangles. It was made using the lost-wax (cire perdue) technique — showing advanced metallurgy. The statue is now in the National Museum, New Delhi. It is one of the most iconic artefacts of the IVC.

q: The famous 'Pashupati Seal' showing a figure seated in a yogic posture was found at which site?
explanation: The 'Pashupati Seal' (also called the Proto-Shiva seal) was found at Mohenjodaro. It shows a figure seated in a yogic posture (padmasana), surrounded by animals (elephant, tiger, rhino, buffalo, deer). The figure has three faces and horns. Some scholars (like John Marshall) identified it as an early form of Shiva (Pashupati, the lord of animals). Others dispute this. The seal suggests that yoga and Shiva worship may have IVC origins.

q: Which IVC site in Gujarat is known as a port city with a dockyard?
explanation: Lothal (Gujarat) is known as a port city with a dockyard — the world's earliest known dockyard (2200 BCE). The dockyard was connected to the Sabarmati river (for trade with Mesopotamia via the Arabian Sea). Lothal also had: (1) a bead factory; (2) a warehouse; (3) rice cultivation. Lothal was discovered in 1954 by S.R. Rao. It shows that the IVC had maritime trade with Mesopotamia (Sumer).

q: Which IVC site is known for its sophisticated water management system with reservoirs?
explanation: Dholavira (Gujarat, Kutch district) is known for its sophisticated water management system — with a series of large reservoirs (some up to 73m long) connected by channels. The city was divided into three parts (citadel, middle town, lower town). Dholavira also had a large signboard with IVC script (the first known signboard). Dholavira is a UNESCO World Heritage Site (2021). It shows advanced water conservation in a semi-arid region.

q: Which IVC site is known for evidence of fire altars?
explanation: Kalibangan (Rajasthan, on the banks of the Ghaggar river) is known for evidence of fire altars — suggesting ritual fire worship. Kalibangan also had: (1) ploughed fields (the earliest evidence of agriculture in the IVC); (2) a unique 'fire altar' in the citadel; (3) a lower town with a grid plan. Kalibangan means 'black bangles' (from the numerous terracotta bangles found there). It was discovered by Amlanand Ghosh (1951).

q: The IVC site in Haryana which is the largest IVC site in the Indian subcontinent is:
explanation: Rakhigarhi (Haryana) is the largest IVC site in the Indian subcontinent — larger than Mohenjodaro (which was previously thought to be the largest). Rakhigarhi covers about 350 hectares (Mohenjodaro covers about 200 hectares). It was excavated by Amarendra Nath (1997-2000) and Vasant Shinde (2011-2016). DNA studies of Rakhigarhi skeletons (2019) showed that the IVC people had a mix of Iranian hunter-gatherer and South Asian hunter-gatherer ancestry — no steppe (Aryan) ancestry.

q: The decline of the Indus Valley Civilisation is attributed to which of the following?
options: All of the above (climate change, river shifts, Aryan invasion theory, trade decline)
explanation: The decline of the IVC (c. 1900-1750 BCE) is attributed to multiple factors: (1) climate change (drying of the Saraswati/Ghaggar river; aridification); (2) river shifts (the Indus and Ghaggar changed course); (3) the Aryan invasion theory (now contested — many scholars reject it); (4) decline of trade with Mesopotamia; (5) ecological degradation (deforestation, overgrazing); (6) floods and earthquakes. The most widely accepted theory is climate change and river shifts (not invasion). The IVC did not disappear suddenly — it declined gradually (late Harappan phase, 1900-1750 BCE).

q: The IVC people traded with which ancient civilisation?
explanation: The IVC people traded with Mesopotamia (Sumer/Akkad) — the earliest known long-distance maritime trade. Mesopotamian texts mention 'Meluhha' (believed to be the IVC) as a trading partner. IVC goods found in Mesopotamia: carnelian beads, cotton, ivory, copper. Mesopotamian goods found in IVC: cylinder seals. The trade was conducted via the Arabian Sea — Lothal was the main port. Dilmun (Bahrain) and Magan (Oman) were intermediaries.

q: The IVC people primarily cultivated which crops?
explanation: The IVC people primarily cultivated wheat and barley (the staple crops). They also cultivated: (1) cotton (the IVC was the first to cultivate cotton — the Greeks called it 'sindon' after Sindh); (2) sesame; (3) peas; (4) dates; (5) rice (at Lothal, in Gujarat). The IVC used ploughs (evidence at Kalibangan) and had a sophisticated agricultural system. The granary at Mohenjodaro (the largest in the ancient world) stored surplus grain.

q: The IVC was the first to produce which commodity in the world?
explanation: The IVC was the first to cultivate and produce cotton in the world. The Greeks called cotton 'sindon' (after Sindh) and 'karpass' (after the Sanskrit karpasa). Cotton fragments have been found at Mohenjodaro. The cultivation of cotton was one of the IVC's most important contributions to the world — it spread to Mesopotamia, Egypt, and eventually Europe. The IVC also produced high-quality beads (carnelian), pottery, and bronze tools.

q: Which animal is most frequently depicted on IVC seals?
explanation: The unicorn (one-horned bull) is the most frequently depicted animal on IVC seals — about 60% of all seals. Other animals depicted: elephant, tiger, rhino, buffalo, crocodile, deer. The 'Pashupati Seal' (with the yogic figure surrounded by animals) is also famous. The animals suggest that the IVC people lived in a diverse ecological zone (including elephants, tigers, rhinos — which are now found in more limited areas).

q: The IVC site at Banawali (Haryana) is known for:
options: Evidence of both pre-Harappan and Harappan culture
explanation: Banawali (Haryana, on the banks of the Saraswati river) is known for evidence of both pre-Harappan (early) and Harappan (mature) culture — showing a continuity of settlement. Banawali had: (1) a fortified town; (2) a citadel; (3) a lower town; (4) evidence of both barley and rice cultivation. The site shows the transition from rural to urban life in the IVC. It was excavated by R.S. Bisht (1974).

q: The IVC site at Chanhudaro (Sindh) is known for:
explanation: Chanhudaro (Sindh, about 130 km south of Mohenjodaro) is known for its bead-making factory — the earliest known bead factory in the world. The IVC was famous for its carnelian beads, which were exported to Mesopotamia. Chanhudaro also had: (1) a shell-working industry; (2) a pottery workshop; (3) evidence of craft specialisation. The city was not fortified (unlike most IVC cities). It was excavated by Ernest Mackay (1931).

q: Which IVC site in Bihar shows evidence of late/post-Harappan culture?
options: None — the IVC did not extend to Bihar
explanation: The IVC did NOT extend to Bihar — it was concentrated in the Indus Valley (Pakistan), Gujarat, Rajasthan, Haryana, and Punjab. The easternmost IVC sites are in Uttar Pradesh (Alamgirpur, near Meerut). Bihar was not part of the IVC — Bihar's prehistoric culture was the Chalcolithic (Chirand) and later the Chalcolithic/Iron Age culture. Bihar entered the historical period with the rise of the Mahajanapadas (Magadha) around 600 BCE.

q: The Indus Valley Civilisation's political organisation is believed to have been:
options: A decentralised, egalitarian society without a king
explanation: The IVC's political organisation is believed to have been a decentralised, egalitarian society without a king — based on the absence of: (1) palaces; (2) royal burials; (3) weapons; (4) large temples (except the Great Bath); (5) statues of rulers. The cities appear to have been governed by a council of merchants or priests. However, since the script is undeciphered, this is speculative. Some scholars argue for a priest-king (the 'Priest-King' statue of Mohenjodaro) but this is debated.

q: The 'Priest-King' statue was found at which IVC site?
explanation: The 'Priest-King' statue (a 17.5 cm soapstone head with a fillet/band around the head, and a robe with trefoil pattern) was found at Mohenjodaro (1925). The statue is called the 'Priest-King' because it was thought to represent a religious leader — but this identification is debated. The statue is now in the National Museum of Pakistan, Karachi. It is one of the most iconic IVC artefacts — though its significance remains uncertain due to the undeciphered script.

q: Arrange the IVC sites from north to south: 1. Rakhigarhi 2. Kalibangan 3. Mohenjodaro 4. Lothal
explanation: North to south: Rakhigarhi (Haryana, ~29°N) → Kalibangan (Rajasthan, ~28°N) → Mohenjodaro (Sindh, ~27°N) → Lothal (Gujarat, ~22°N). The IVC extended from Sutkagendor (Pakistan-Iran border) in the west to Alamgirpur (UP, near Meerut) in the east; and from Manda (Jammu) in the north to Daimabad (Maharashtra) in the south. Total area: about 1.3 million sq km — the largest of the ancient civilisations.
